source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

claim_chain: Circulatory IgA is regularly assumed to be monomeric, but we observed J-coupled dimers representing up to ~30% of total serum IgA
Mass spectrometry-based clonal profiling revealed three populations: monomer-only, dimer-only, and co-occurring clones
Structurally promiscuous clones dominated both individuals' serum IgA1 clonal repertoires
Co-occurring clones exhibited bimodal SEC distributions diagnostic of structural promiscuity
Every unique IgA1 clone could potentially be produced as a variable mixture of monomers and J-coupled dimers
The human immune system apparently prefers producing both forms in parallel

typical_pattern: Present contrasting observation from current study
Call for future work or broader validation

likely_priorities: Validation of clonal assignment methodology and stringency of mass/RT matching criteria
Exclusion of methodological artifacts from SEC coelution or differential PTM
Direct cellular evidence for co-occurring clone production
Cohort size and generalizability beyond two donors
likely_attacks: Small cohort size limits generalizability of findings
Protein-centric approach cannot establish cellular origins directly
Potential for SEC peak overlap causing artificial co-occurring clone assignment
Differential cleavage efficiency or PTM could skew assembly ratios
Serum half-life differences may inflate apparent monomer abundance
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The discussion extensively addresses methodological concerns: 'Evaluation of Potential Methodological Biases' section discusses SEC coelution artifacts, differential PTM, unequal Fab release, and detection limits. The authors acknowledge 'A limitation of our protein-centric approach is that the cellular origins of these co-occurring IgA1 antibody clones cannot be directly established.' They note 'These results have potentially significant implications for our understanding of human IgA but are currently limited to the small cohort of this study. Analysis of a larger, more diverse donor cohort is needed.' The paper systematically defends against each potential artifact while acknowledging where uncertainties remain.
